paragraphs.body: In Bird By Bird, Anne Lamott drives home that, in fiction writing, characters are the story. Creating a fully fleshed out character writes the story for you. She writes, “Plot grows out of character. If you focus on who the people in your story are…something is bound to happen.” (pg. 52). Throughout the semester, through my reading and writing, I’ve discovered this to be completely true. The importance of character was by far the most helpful thing I learned this semester. In my writing, I’ve found that spending more time creating a character and tweaking them before writing any actual plot leads to a much more successful story than writing a plot and trying to create a character out of that.

annotations.comment: I love this quote — it proves true for me often.

paragraphs.body: For my first flash fiction, I came up with a title and spent days creating a character. Once I really knew her, the story virtually wrote itself in just a few hours. My professor had provided the class with two lists of questions to help us get to know ourn characters, from the inside out and the outside in. I copied and pasted these questions into a doc and used them to create a believable and hopefully relatable character. In her chapter on character, Lamott explains that characters can be messy, contradictory, and complex, because real people are too. But they have to remain believable because this makes the story believable. When figuring out what this character wants, I had to think of a realistic want and when figuring out what’s preventing her from having this, I had to think of a realistic obstacle. Deciding what this character looked like was one thing, but figuring out how she looks compared to the people around her and how much effort she puts into her appearance were the questions that made this character come to life for me.

paragraphs.body: Once I knew all these things about her, I could put her into whatever situation I wanted and I knew how she would act and what she would think about the whole thing, just like Lamott had promised! As I began writing, I took on this tone that I hadn’t ever used before because it worked with how I thought the character’s internal monologue was like. I think understanding the character before writing was incredibly impactful because the tone was the most unique thing about the story and I would have never thought of using it unless I created Sloan the stoner from Boulder. I wrote about an insane situation but I think it remained realistic because the character was well thought out and was based on stereotypes many people could picture, so the weird circumstances made more sense.

annotations.comment: Yeah, and it was very believable and fun.

paragraphs.body: I saw this play out in other stories I read throughout the semester. The Apartment, one of the very first short stories assigned, was my favorite short story because it’s driven entirely by the detailed information about both characters. The situation is funny and the characters’ relationship with each other is so interesting, but the detailed descriptions of what the characters get up to when they’re alone is what makes the story so, so good. It’s especially effective because the perspective switches back and forth, so the author explores both characters' inner thoughts about the other. I really loved how it was written and I could definitely tell that the author, T.C. Boyle, spent time with the characters he created. While based on real people, Boyle created fully fleshed out characters which made The Apartment more interesting than reading a historical account of what actually happened.

annotations.comment: It’s so good — it really is just about character driving plot. Brilliantly done.
annotations.context_left: ed, was my favorite short story because it’s driven entirely by the detailed information about both

annotations.comment: Agreed. I think that using real people as his inspiration actually made the work harder — he couldn’t just make stuff up entirely.

paragraphs.body: Another short story with wonderfully written characters is Emma Cline’s The Nanny. The book starts in the aftermath of some event and slowly reveals what went down through Kayla’s flashbacks and introspection. At the beginning of the story, the only information I got was Kayla’s personality and her interpretation of her surroundings. She is very observant about the people she interacts with, which adds layers to the situation once the backstory is revealed. She was a famous actor’s live-in nanny for his young son and she was the actor’s mistress. She thinks she’s a bad person because she knew what she was doing was obviously wrong, but her perceptiveness also meant she knew when producers or other staff knew she was sleeping with Rafe, the actor. When the story breaks and she has to go into hiding, she reflects on these instances where she saw people smirk and reveal that they knew, which makes her feel even worse about how things went down. But the best thing about how Kayla was written was that she never alludes to any regret.

annotations.comment: Yes. I love/hate that story. It’s beautifully written and Kayla drives me insane.

paragraphs.body: Her complicated feelings about the affair and her current living conditions, hauled up with her mom’s friend, highlights what Lamott was getting at in Bird By Bird: characters should be messy, complicated, contradictory, and sometimes straight up bad people, because that’s what makes them accurate depictions of real people. I think, in a way, I wanted to hear that Rafe coerced her into the relationship or that she tried to break things off or that she truly felt sorry for what she did to Henry, the child, and Jessica, the wife. Before I knew the backstory, I liked Kayla a lot and I was holding out for an explanation for the affair yet it never came. I don’t think I would ever think to write a character like that, who goes against the instincts or wants that I personally have, but Cline’s story was so good and I really think it boils down to Kayla subverting the typical “other woman” narrative.  Lamott also praises revealing who a character is slowly and The Nanny exemplifies why this is such a good technique. Kayla is complicated but I didn’t know that all at once, I learned it as I read and as I cringed at her attitude.

annotations.comment: Yeah, that’s a great connection. She is complicated and both likable and unlikable.
annotations.context_left: as I read and as I

paragraphs.body: I learned a lot about writing during my 15 weeks in Intro to Fiction Writing, but the biggest change in my writing, the most helpful element of a story to concentrate on was, for me, character. At the beginning of the year, I had no idea of what I wanted to write about and I was least confident in my ability to create a realistic and interesting character. And while I don’t know how much fiction writing I’ll do beyond the context of this class, I ended up being really happy with and proud of the work I produced this semester.

annotations.comment: You’re quite a good writer, Enzo — I hope you’ll keep doing it in some way. Thank you for your hard work! (late)
